annotations.text: The table underscores that the constellation of a **hypervascular mass with a central scar**, **normal liver enzymes**, **normal tumor markers**, and **systemic constitutional symptoms** is most parsimoniously explained by **fibrolamellar hepatocellular carcinoma**.

The only serious rival is an **inflammatory pseudotumor**, which can mimic those imaging features and clinical signs, but the size and the classic central scar pattern on delayed imaging are more in line with FL‑HCC.
nstitutional symptoms are thought to be mediated by cytokine release from the tumor.

the standard of care is **surgical resection** (anatomic left‑lobe hepatectomy or extended left hepatectomy) because the tumor typically arises in a non‑cirrhotic liver and patients tolerate major hepatectomy well.
The presence of a solitary lesion, absence of vascular invasion on imaging, and lack of extra‑hepatic disease make the patient a good surgical candidate.
The imaging hallmark of a hypervascular lesion with a central scar, coupled with normal liver function and systemic inflammatory symptoms, points most strongly toward fibrolamellar HCC.
The clinical picture (intermittent fevers, weight loss) aligns with an inflammatory pseudotumor.
The patient’s age and sex fit the epidemiology of FNH, and the imaging description of a hypervascular mass with a central scar is reminiscent of FNH.
